---
title: "Measurement Error in Causal Inference"
author: "Bielicke, L. and Xochihua-Tlecuitl, T."
date: today
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: false
    self-contained: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from IPython.display import display, HTML

def show(fig):
    display(HTML(fig.to_html(full_html=False, include_plotlyjs="cdn")))

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
```

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Key Ideas

By the end of this tutorial you should be able to:

1. Define *classical* measurement error 
2. Describe how classical measurement error influences treatment, conditioning,
and outcome variables differently
4. Apply instrumental variables and 2SLS to correct for measurement bias
5. TODO
6. TODO
:::

---

## 1. Measurement Error: Definitions & Types
**Measurement
error** is the misalignment between the true state of a variable and its reported
state. We often assume that the variables we reason about are the variables
we observe. This assumption rarely holds in practive. Instead, we observe some
*proxy variable*
i.e., a noisy or imperfect stand-in for the true variable of interest. 

Understanding measurement error is important because the variable we draw
conclusions *with* is not always the variable we draw conclusions *about*.
To identify unbiased causal estimates, we need to understadn the relationship
between the variable of interest and the noisy proxy. 


### 1.1 Classical Measurement Error
Classical measurement error occurs when the observed variable equals the the true value **plus**
some noise:

$$X^* = X + \varepsilon$$

where the error term $\varepsilon$ satisfies four key assumptions:

- $\mathbb{E}(\varepsilon) = 0$ (zero mean)
- $\varepsilon \perp X$ (independent of the true value)
- $\varepsilon \perp Y$ (independent of the outcome)
- $\varepsilon \perp Z$ (independent of any covariate)

## Question
What does the first assumption imply about the relationship between
$\mathbb{E}(\varepsilon \mid X)$ and $\mathbb{E}(\varepsilon \mid X^*)$?

```{dot}
//| label: fig-dag-basic
//| fig-cap: "We observe Y* (the proxy) but want to reason about the latent Y. The error U_Y* enters only through Y* and is independent of X."
//| fig-height: 1.6
digraph {
  rankdir=LR
  node [fontname="Helvetica" fontsize=13]
  X      [shape=square]
  Y      [shape=circle style=dashed]
  Ystar  [shape=square  label="Y*"]
  UYstar [shape=circle  style=dashed label="U_Y*"]
  X -> Y
  Y -> Ystar
  UYstar -> Ystar [style=dashed]
}
```

There are other types of measurmeemnt error (e.g., differential). We will focus
on classicial measurment error throughout this tutorial. 

### 2 Motivating Example: Subjective Well-Being

Researchers are studying the effect of happiness on physical activity. Because
they cannot measure partipants' happiness directly, they use a self-reported to
determine happiness. The researchers believe that the self-reports, though not a
perfect representation of happiness, correlate with partipants true happiness
levels. 

## Note 
Prior work shows that single scale surveys correlate with true metrics
at roughly $r = 0.5$, while multi-item scales reaches about $r = 0.8$. Neither
is perfect. 

## Other Examples of Measurement Error

- Blood pressure recorded at a single clinic visit
- Self-reported hours worked
- Human error when reading time from a clock
- Mechanical error from scales

---

## 3. Causal Estimand, Identification & Attenuation Bias

### 3.1 Causal Setup & Identification

Throughout the tutorial, we are interested in measuring the average treatment
effect (ATE) of $X$ on $Y$. Most of our results assume a linear regression
model. Below, we explain how measurement error in treatment, conditioning, and
outcome variables can influence findings.

### 3.2 Classical ME in Treatment: Attenuation Bias
We want to determine $\beta$ in $Y = \beta X + \varepsilon$, but we
observe only $X^* = X + U$. Plugging the proxy
into OLS gives:

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y,\, X^*)}{\text{Var}(X^*)}
= \frac{\text{Cov}(\beta X + \varepsilon,\; X + U)}{\text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(U)}
= \frac{\beta\,\text{Var}(X)}{\text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(U)}
= \beta\lambda$$

where $\lambda = \frac{\text{Var}(X)}{\text{Var}(X) +
\text{Var}(U)}$ is the **reliability ratio**. In the derivation above, we leverage the classical measurement error assumptions
that $\text{Cov}(X, U) = 0$ and $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon, U) = 0$.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## In which direction is $\hat{\beta}$ biased when there is classical measurement error in $X$?

Because $\lambda < 1$, the OLS estimate attenuates toward zero. With $\lambda =
0.5$, a true effect of $\beta = 0.6$ appears as $\hat{\beta} \approx 0.3$.

:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Attenuation Bias

Attenuation bias occurs when a regressor's estimated coefficient is biased
toward zero.

:::

Explore the chart below to see how the reliability ratio affects our causal
estimate. Notice that when the reliability ratio gets smaller, the fitted slope
shrinks toward zero even as the true relationship stays fixed. In other words,
the causal estimate *attenuates*.



```{python}
#| label: fig-attenuation
#| fig-cap: "Attenuation bias as reliability decreases. The green line is the true slope; the red dashed line is what OLS recovers from the noisy proxy."

n = 500
beta_true = 0.6
lambdas = np.round(np.arange(0.10, 1.01, 0.05), 2)
xline = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 120)

frames, steps = [], []
for lam in lambdas:
    var_u = (1 - lam) / lam
    X_true = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
    U      = rng.normal(0, np.sqrt(var_u), n)
    X_obs  = X_true + U
    Y      = beta_true * X_true + rng.normal(0, 1, n)
    b_hat  = np.cov(Y, X_obs)[0, 1] / np.var(X_obs)
    b_th   = beta_true * lam

    frames.append(go.Frame(
        name=str(lam),
        data=[
            go.Scatter(x=X_obs, y=Y, mode="markers",
                       marker=dict(size=3, color="steelblue", opacity=0.25),
                       showlegend=False),
            go.Scatter(x=xline, y=beta_true * xline, mode="lines",
                       name=f"True β = {beta_true}",
                       line=dict(color="green", width=2.5)),
            go.Scatter(x=xline, y=b_hat * xline, mode="lines",
                       name=f"OLS β̂ = {b_hat:.3f}",
                       line=dict(color="crimson", width=2.5, dash="dash")),
            go.Scatter(x=xline, y=b_th * xline, mode="lines",
                       name=f"Theory βλ = {b_th:.3f}",
                       line=dict(color="darkorange", width=1.5, dash="dot")),
        ]
    ))
    steps.append(dict(
        method="animate",
        label=str(lam),
        args=[[str(lam)], dict(mode="immediate",
                               frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True),
                               transition=dict(duration=0))]
    ))

# start at λ=1.0 (last frame)
init = frames[-1].data
fig_att = go.Figure(data=init, frames=frames)
fig_att.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(title="Observed X*", range=[-4, 4]),
    yaxis=dict(title="Y", range=[-4, 4]),
    legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.7)"),
    margin=dict(t=40, b=80),
    sliders=[dict(
        active=len(lambdas) - 1,
        currentvalue=dict(prefix="Reliability λ = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
        pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
        steps=steps
    )],
    height=440,
)
show(fig_att)
```

### 3.3 ME in Outcome Variables
We *still* want to determine $\beta$ in $Y = \beta X + \varepsilon$. This time, we
observe a noisy outcome $Y^* = Y + U$. Plugging the proxy
into OLS gives:

$$Y^* = \beta X + (\varepsilon + U)$$

**What happens to $\hat\beta_X$?**

$$\hat\beta_X = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y^*,\, X)}{\text{Var}(X)}
= \frac{\text{Cov}(Y + U,\, X)}{\text{Var}(X)}
= \frac{\text{Cov}(Y, X) + \text{Cov}(U, X)}{\text{Var}(X)}$$

Under classical ME, $U \perp X$, so $\text{Cov}(U, X) = 0$:

$$\hat\beta_X = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y, X)}{\text{Var}(X)} = \beta$$

The measurement noise is absorbed entirely into the composite residual
$\varepsilon + U$. Because $U$ is independent of $X$, it does not distort the
signal between $X$ and $Y$, so the coefficient estimate is **unbiased**.
However, the residual variance increases from $\sigma^2_\varepsilon$ to
$\sigma^2_\varepsilon + \sigma^2_U$, which inflates standard errors and reduces
$R^2$.

### Exercise: Outcome measurement error
The following exersize shows how analysis with a noisy measure of $Y$ compares
to the ground truth. The `cars` dataset (built into base R) records stopping
distance ($Y$, in feet) for 50 cars traveling at various speeds ($X$, in mph).
Suppose a technician records stopping distance with a tape measure that has
random error. We simulate what that technician would have recorded by creating a
new variable where each datapoint is equal to the distance plus some random
noise term. 

<details>
<summary>Load the data in R</summary>

```r
data(cars)
set.seed(5)
cars$dist_noisy <- cars$dist + rnorm(50, mean = 0, sd = 20)
```

</details>


**Question 1.** Compute the measurement error $U = Y^* - Y$ (`dist_noisy` minus
`dist`). What is the mean and standard deviation? 

```r
err <- cars$dist_noisy - cars$dist
mean(err)
sd(err)
```

**Question 2.** Check the correlation between the original `dist` and
`dist_noisy`.

```r
cor(cars$dist, cars$dist_noisy)
```

**Question 3.** Regress stopping distance on speed using the original `dist` and
then using `dist_noisy`. Compare the slope coefficients, standard errors, and
$R^2$ across the two models. 

```r
summary(lm(dist       ~ speed, data = cars))
summary(lm(dist_noisy ~ speed, data = cars))
```

::: {.callout-tip icon=false collapse=true}
## Solution

```r
data(cars)
set.seed(5)
cars$dist_noisy <- cars$dist + rnorm(50, mean = 0, sd = 20)

# error stats
err <- cars$dist_noisy - cars$dist
mean(err)   # ≈ 0.0 ft  — mean-zero, satisfies classical ME
sd(err)     # ≈ 18.5 ft

# correlation
cor(cars$dist, cars$dist_noisy)   # ≈ 0.874

# regression
summary(lm(dist       ~ speed, data = cars))
# slope = 3.93,  SE = 0.42,  R² = 0.651

summary(lm(dist_noisy ~ speed, data = cars))
# slope = 4.02,  SE = 0.58,  R² = 0.499  


```
:::

The two slopes are nearly identical (3.93 vs 4.02). The difference is due to
sampling noise. The standard error grows from 0.42 to 0.58 (+38%) and $R^2$
falls from 0.651 to 0.499. A noisier outcome variable means you need a larger
sample to detect the same true effect. :::

### Simulation

Each point below is one observation plotted as (true $Y$, observed $Y^*$). Drag
the slider to add noise to the outcome variable and notive how the statistics
update: the
mean stays near zero while the standard deviation
grows.

```{python}
#| label: fig-outcome-me
#| fig-cap: "Classical ME in the outcome: true Y (x-axis) vs observed Y* (y-axis). The error mean stays ~0 while the SD grows with noise."

n = 100
beta_true = 0.6
sigma_y_vals = np.round(np.arange(0.0, 2.05, 0.1), 1)
X_ome = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
eps_ome = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
Y_true_ome = beta_true * X_ome + eps_ome

frames_ome, steps_ome = [], []
for sigma in sigma_y_vals:
    U_Y = rng.normal(0, sigma, n) if sigma > 0 else np.zeros(n)
    Y_obs_ome = Y_true_ome + U_Y
    err_mean = float(np.mean(U_Y))
    err_sd   = float(np.std(U_Y))
    corr_yy  = float(np.corrcoef(Y_true_ome, Y_obs_ome)[0, 1])

    frames_ome.append(go.Frame(
        name=str(sigma),
        data=[
            go.Scatter(
                x=Y_true_ome, y=Y_obs_ome, mode="markers",
                marker=dict(size=4, color="steelblue", opacity=0.35),
                name="Observations", showlegend=True,
            ),
            go.Scatter(
                x=[-4, 4], y=[-4, 4], mode="lines",
                name="Perfect measurement",
                line=dict(color="gray", width=1.5, dash="dash"),
                showlegend=True,
            ),
            go.Scatter(
                x=[None], y=[None], mode="markers",
                marker=dict(opacity=0),
                name=f"Error mean = {err_mean:+.3f}   SD = {err_sd:.3f}   r(Y, Y*) = {corr_yy:.3f}",
                showlegend=True,
            ),
        ]
    ))
    steps_ome.append(dict(
        method="animate", label=str(sigma),
        args=[[str(sigma)], dict(mode="immediate",
                                  frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True),
                                  transition=dict(duration=0))]
    ))

fig_ome = go.Figure(data=frames_ome[0].data, frames=frames_ome)
fig_ome.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(title="True outcome  Y", range=[-5, 5]),
    yaxis=dict(title="Observed outcome  Y*", range=[-7, 7]),
    legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.75)"),
    margin=dict(t=40, b=80),
    sliders=[dict(
        active=0,
        currentvalue=dict(prefix="Noise σ = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
        pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
        steps=steps_ome,
    )],
    height=440,
)
show(fig_ome)
```

---

$\beta_Z$ attenuates towards zero, but what happens to $\beta_X$? Can we predict
the direction of bias? 

### 4.1 Bias in Multivariate OLS
The "pure" variation in $Z$ left after removing $X$ is the residual from regressing $Z$ on $X$:

$$\sigma^2_{Z|X} = \text{Var}(Z) - \text{Var}(\hat{Z}_{|X}) \leq \sigma^2_Z$$

The attenuation factor in the multivariate model becomes

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{\sigma^2_{Z|X}}{\sigma^2_{Z|X} + \sigma^2_U}$$

and 
$$\hat\beta_{Z^*} = \lambda_1\,\beta_Z$$

Because $\sigma^2_{Z|X} \leq \sigma^2_Z$ and $Z$ is correlated with $X$, we have
$\lambda_1 \leq \lambda$. In words, classicial measurement error in multivariate
regression accentuates attenuation in the variable with measurement error. 

With a single covariate $Z$, let $\delta$ be the slope from the regression of $Z$ on $X$
(i.e., $\mathbb{E}[Z \mid X] = \delta_0 + \delta X$). Then:

$$\hat\beta_{X} = \beta_X + (1 - \lambda_1)\,\beta_Z\,\delta$$

The bias term $(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta$ is non-zero whenever $X$ and $Z$
are correlated ($\delta \neq 0$) and $X$ has a true effect ($\beta_X \neq 0$).
In other words, measurement error in a conditioning variables biases the
treatment coefficient, and we cannot predict the direction of bias.

The chart below makes this concrete. The true model is $Y = \beta_X X + \beta_Z
Z + \varepsilon$ with $\beta_X = 1$ and $\beta_Z = 0.8$. $X$ and $Z$ are
correlated ($\rho = 0.7$), so a noisy $Z^*$ leaves residual confounding that
flows into $\hat\beta_X$. Drag the slider to reduce the reliability of $Z^*$
and watch $\hat\beta_X$ depart from the truth.

```{python}
#| label: fig-conditioning-me
#| fig-cap: "ME in the conditioning variable. The green line is the true β_X = 1; the red line is what OLS recovers from a noisy Z*. As reliability falls, residual confounding pushes β̂_X upward (because β_Z > 0 and ρ > 0)."

n       = 2000
bX_true = 1.0
bZ      = 0.8
rho     = 0.7   # X–Z correlation (confounding strength)

rng2 = np.random.default_rng(7)

# Generate data once — fixed across slider frames
XZ  = rng2.multivariate_normal([0, 0], [[1, rho], [rho, 1]], n)
X_c = XZ[:, 0]
Z_c = XZ[:, 1]
Y_c = bX_true * X_c + bZ * Z_c + rng2.normal(0, 1, n)

lambdas_c = np.round(np.arange(0.10, 1.01, 0.05), 2)
xline_c   = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 120)
sigma2_ZX = 1 - rho**2   # Var(Z | X) under unit-variance standardization

frames_c, steps_c = [], []
for lam in lambdas_c:
    var_u = (1 - lam) / lam
    U_c   = rng2.normal(0, np.sqrt(var_u), n)
    Zs    = Z_c + U_c

    A     = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), X_c, Zs])
    coefs = np.linalg.lstsq(A, Y_c, rcond=None)[0]
    bX_hat = coefs[1]

    # Theoretical prediction
    lam1    = sigma2_ZX / (sigma2_ZX + var_u)
    bX_th   = bX_true + (1 - lam1) * bZ * rho

    frames_c.append(go.Frame(
        name=str(lam),
        data=[
            go.Scatter(x=X_c, y=Y_c, mode="markers",
                       marker=dict(size=3, color="steelblue", opacity=0.15),
                       showlegend=False),
            go.Scatter(x=xline_c, y=bX_true * xline_c, mode="lines",
                       name=f"True β_X = {bX_true}",
                       line=dict(color="green", width=2.5)),
            go.Scatter(x=xline_c, y=bX_hat * xline_c, mode="lines",
                       name=f"OLS β̂_X = {bX_hat:.3f}",
                       line=dict(color="crimson", width=2.5, dash="dash")),
            go.Scatter(x=xline_c, y=bX_th * xline_c, mode="lines",
                       name=f"Theory = {bX_th:.3f}",
                       line=dict(color="darkorange", width=1.5, dash="dot")),
        ]
    ))
    steps_c.append(dict(
        method="animate",
        label=str(lam),
        args=[[str(lam)], dict(mode="immediate",
                               frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True),
                               transition=dict(duration=0))]
    ))

init_c = frames_c[-1].data
fig_c  = go.Figure(data=init_c, frames=frames_c)
fig_c.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(title="X (Treatment)", range=[-4, 4]),
    yaxis=dict(title="Y", range=[-5, 5]),
    legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.7)"),
    margin=dict(t=40, b=80),
    sliders=[dict(
        active=len(lambdas_c) - 1,
        currentvalue=dict(prefix="Reliability λ(Z*) = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
        pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
        steps=steps_c
    )],
    height=440,
)
show(fig_c)
```

---

## 5. Instrumental Variables (IV) & 2SLS

So, now you know that measurment error causes problems... what can you do to
accounnt for the bias caused by measurement error? 

### IV for ME in Treatment Variables
To recover $\beta$ consistently despite noisy $X^*$, we need a second proxy
$T$ for the true $X$. The IV must satisfy three assumptions: 

1. **Exclusion**: $\text{Cov}(T, \varepsilon) = ($T$ affects $Y$ only
   through $X$)
2. **Independence**: $U_T \perp U_{X^*}$ (measurement errors in the two
   proxies are independent)
3. **Relevance**: $\text{Cov}(T, X^*) \neq 0$ ($T$ is correlated with
   the mismeasured treatment)

Remember that we want to measure $\beta$ in We want to determine $\beta$ in $Y =
\beta X + \varepsilon$ and we measure a noisy $X^* = X + U$. Then 

$$\text{Cov}(Y, Z) = \text{Cov}(\beta X + \varepsilon, Z) = \beta \text{Cov}(X, Z) 
$$

$$\text{Cov}(X^*, Z) = \text{Cov}(\beta X + U + \varepsilon, Z) =\text{Cov}(X, Z) 
$$

so $\hat{\beta} = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y, T)}{\text{Cov}(X^*, T)}$. In practice, this estimator is implemented as 2SLS. Let $T$ be a valid instrument.

**Stage 1**: regress $X^*$ on $T$:

$$\hat X^* = \hat\pi_0 + \hat\pi_1 T$$

**Stage 2**: regress $Y$ on $\hat X^*$:

$$Y = \alpha + \beta\,\hat X^* + \eta$$

### Exercise: BMI and Blood Pressure

Higher BMI is a well-established risk factor for hypertension. A researcher
wants to quantify how much systolic blood pressure rises per unit increase in
BMI, using NHANES survey data. BMI is calculated from height and weight
measurements, both of which carry small random errors (scale calibration,
rounding). We simulate two independent BMI readings as proxies for each
participant's true BMI — one used as the regressor, the other as the instrument:

$$\text{BMI}^*_1 = \text{BMI} + U_1 \qquad \text{BMI}^*_2 = \text{BMI} + U_2
\qquad U_1 \perp U_2$$

With $\sigma_U = 4\ \text{kg/m}^2$ and $\text{SD}(\text{BMI}) \approx 7\
\text{kg/m}^2$, the reliability ratio is $\lambda = 7^2/(7^2 + 4^2) \approx
0.75$.

#### Step 0: Load data and simulate measurement error

```r
library(NHANES)
d <- na.omit(NHANES[, c("BPSysAve", "BMI")])

set.seed(9)
d$bmi_star <- d$BMI + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 4)  # Reading 1 (noisy X*)
d$bmi_iv   <- d$BMI + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 4)  # Reading 2 (instrument)
```

#### Step 1: OLS with the noisy proxy

```r
ols_fit <- lm(BPSysAve ~ bmi_star, data = d)
summary(ols_fit)
```

OLS recovers a slope of ~0.48, well below the benchmark of ~0.64 from the
un-corrupted `BMI` column — attenuation of ~26%, consistent with $\lambda
\approx 0.75$.

#### Step 2: Manual 2SLS

**Stage 1** — regress the noisy regressor on the instrument:

```r
stage1    <- lm(bmi_star ~ bmi_iv, data = d)
summary(stage1)$r.squared   # ≈ 0.57 — very strong first stage (F > 10,000)
```

**Stage 2** — regress blood pressure on the fitted BMI from stage 1:

```r
d$bmi_hat <- fitted(stage1)
stage2    <- lm(BPSysAve ~ bmi_hat, data = d)
```

Note: standard errors from manual stage 2 are incorrect. Use `ivreg()`.

#### Step 3: 2SLS with `ivreg()`

```r
library(AER)
iv_fit <- ivreg(BPSysAve ~ bmi_star | bmi_iv, data = d)
summary(iv_fit)
```

#### Step 4: Compare

```r
library(stargazer)
stargazer(ols_fit, stage2, iv_fit,
          type          = "text",
          column.labels = c("OLS (attenuated)", "Manual 2SLS", "ivreg 2SLS"),
          dep.var.labels = "Systolic BP (mmHg)",
          covariate.labels = c("BMI* (noisy)", "BMI (fitted)"),
          keep.stat     = c("n", "rsq"))
```

::: {.callout-tip icon=false collapse=true}
## Expected results

| | OLS | Manual 2SLS | `ivreg` 2SLS |
|---|---|---|---|
| BMI | 0.48 | 0.65 | 0.65 |
| SE | (0.023) | (wrong) | (0.030) |
| $R^2$ | 0.050 | — | — |

OLS attenuates the slope from the benchmark (~0.64) to 0.48 — a 26% shrinkage
consistent with $\lambda \approx 0.75$. Both 2SLS approaches recover 0.65,
almost exactly matching the un-corrupted estimate. The stage-1 $F$-statistic
exceeds 10,000, confirming the instrument is very strong.
:::

---

## 6. Latent Variable Methods: SEM, Scoring & IRT


---

## 7. Measurement Invariance

---

## 8. Takeaways

- Measurement error is not a nuisance to note in a limitations section; it is a
  structural feature of the data-generating process with identifiable causal
  consequences.
- Classical error in the *treatment* attenuates coefficients toward zero by the
  reliability ratio $\lambda$. Classical error in the *outcome* inflates standard
  errors but leaves point estimates unbiased.
- Error in a *conditioning variable* leaves an open backdoor path and can bias
  estimates in either direction.
- Instrumental variables and SEM each offer consistent estimation under different
  assumptions. The choice depends on whether a valid second proxy or a full
  measurement model is available.
- Before worrying about confounding or external validity, ask whether the measures
  themselves are valid proxies for the constructs of interest.

---

## Sources

An, L. (2025). *Measurement in K-12 policy analysis* (Doctoral dissertation, Harvard University). <https://dash.harvard.edu/server/api/core/bitstreams/7035e654-685f-4d1a-837e-e66ab492c665/content>

Bulbulia, J. A. (2024). Methods in causal inference. Part 3: Measurement error and external validity threats. *Evolutionary Human Sciences*, *6*, e42. <https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4723683/pdf/dyu272.pdf>

de Romémont, J. (2024). *Measurement in data science* [Lecture notes]. <https://uclspp.github.io/POLS0013/2-measurement-theory-and-error.html>

Edwards, J. K., Cole, S. R., & Westreich, D. (2015). All your data are always missing: incorporating bias due to measurement error into the potential outcomes framework. *International Journal of Epidemiology*, *44*(4), 1452–1459. <https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11588564/>

Jovanović, M. (n.d.). *Measurement error*. bmbstats Book. <https://mladenjovanovic.github.io/bmbstats-book/measurement-error.html>

Lauderdale, B. E. (in press). *Pragmatic social measurement*. <https://uclspp.github.io/POLS0013/00_textbook/pragmatic-social-measurement.pdf>

Lemieux, T. (2019). *ECON 594: Lecture 8* [Lecture notes]. University of British Columbia. <https://econ2017.sites.olt.ubc.ca/files/2019/05/pdf_594lecture8_thomas-lemieux.pdf>

Li, F. (2024). *Instrumental variables* [Lecture slides]. Duke University. <https://www2.stat.duke.edu/~fl35/teaching/640/Chapter6.1_InstrumentalVariables.pdf>

Machielsen, B. (n.d.). *Measurement error*. <https://basmachielsen.nl/posts/measurement_error>

Hanck, C., Arnold, M., Gerber, A., & Schmelzer, M. (n.d.). *Instrumental variables regression*. In *Introduction to econometrics with R* (Ch. 12). <https://www.econometrics-with-r.org/12-ivr.html>

Pearl, J. (2012). On measurement bias in causal inference. *arXiv:1203.3504*. <https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1203.3504>

Pischke, S. (2007). *Lecture notes on measurement error* [Lecture notes]. London School of Economics. <https://econ.lse.ac.uk/staff/spischke/ec524/Merr_new.pdf>

Rubin, E. (n.d.). *ARE 212: Section 11 — Measurement error* [Course notes]. University of California, Berkeley. <https://edrub.in/ARE212/section11.html#measurement_error>

The Econometrics Blog. (n.d.). *Beyond classical measurement error*. <https://www.econometrics.blog/post/beyond-classical-measurement-error/>
